raw_consumer_statement:
On XX/XX/XXXX I was charged {$760.00} by a merchant listed as XXXX XXXX XXXX after purchasing tickets online for the XXXXXXXX XXXX XXXXXXXX at the XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. 

On XX/XX/XXXX I contacted the XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX  directly by phone because I had questions about the tickets. The venue informed me that : Tickets were not yet on sale They do not sell tickets through third-party vendors Any party selling tickets at that time was fraudulent I immediately contacted American Express and disputed the charge. American Express initially approved the dispute and issued a provisional credit. 

XXXX weeks later, American Express reversed the credit despite acknowledging that no tickets were ever delivered to me no seat numbers, no barcodes, no confirmation, no transfer link, and no proof of fulfillment of any kind. 

American Express stated that the merchant was allowed until XX/XX/XXXX to provide the tickets and that I could not dispute the charge again until after the event date. They also stated that this did not qualify as fraud because I voluntarily provided my credit card number, even though the transaction was induced under false pretenses. 

This is incorrect under the Fair Credit Billing Act. The merchant has no authority to sell tickets for this event, and American Express accepted the word of a fraudulent seller over the venue itself while providing no evidence of delivery. 

American Express failed to properly investigate this dispute, reversed a provisional credit without proof of delivery, and misapplied federal consumer protection law by forcing me to wait until after a future event for goods the merchant is incapable of providing. 

I am requesting that American Express be required to permanently reverse this charge and correct their dispute handling procedures.
